items.title: Flow orchestrator — autonomous dev cycle after human approval
items.description:
The tribunal brain is state-of-the-art at deciding WHAT to work on. But once work starts, Flow goes blind. Every lane transition requires a human to pull. This item makes Flow active: a persistent orchestrator that understands the full development cycle from the board's lane config and drives work forward autonomously.

**The human contract:**
Humans do one thing: approve an item to start (`ready`). Everything after that is Flow's responsibility until the item is `done` or hits a blocker that genuinely needs a human decision.

**How it works:**
Flow runs as a persistent process watching `backlog.json` and `handoff_results/`. On any state change, it:
1. Reads the board's `config.statuses` — the lane labels are the development cycle
2. Reasons about what each lane means (coding, review, testing, deploy — whatever the team configured)
3. Identifies what needs to happen next for every in-flight item
4. Initiates the right agent for the current lane with full context
5. Processes the agent's result and advances the item to the next lane
6. On rejection/blocker: writes a thread, moves item back, re-initiates the responsible agent
7. On stale items: escalates with context

**Semantic lane understanding:**
Flow doesn't need explicit config like `qa watches code-review`. It reads the lane label and reasons from it — the same way a human engineer knows what 'code review' means without being told. This means it works for any workflow: `in-progress → QA → staging → done` is understood just as naturally as `in-progress → code-review → done`.

**Agent initiation:**
When an item enters a lane, Flow picks the best-fit agent from `config.agents` using the tribunal's agent_fit lens — skills, workload, history. It invokes them via `backlog handoff` (for work lanes) or `backlog review` (for review lanes), passing full context including what the previous agent did.

**Human re-engagement (only when necessary):**
- A blocker the agent cannot resolve
- An item reopened 3+ times (reopen_count threshold)
- A policy violation that requires a decision
- No agents available with the right skills

Everything else: Flow handles it.

**Acceptance criteria:**
- `backlog orchestrate` starts the persistent process
- Human approves item to `ready` → orchestrator picks it up, assigns agent, invokes handoff — no further human action needed
- Agent completes → result processed → item advances to next lane → next agent initiated automatically
- Review agent rejects → thread written → item back to in-progress → original agent re-initiated with rejection context
- Item reaches terminal lane (`done`) → orchestrator logs completion, surfaces summary
- Works with any lane config — no hardcoded lane names
- Human is only re-engaged when genuinely blocked

links.reason: Handoff is the execution primitive the orchestrator uses to initiate agents
Result ingestion is how the orchestrator processes what agents produce and closes the loop

items.title: Enforce review gate regardless of lane config
items.description:
Removing a code-review lane from the board config should not silently remove the code review practice. Flow must treat peer review as a hard quality gate — not a lane feature.

**The rule:** Before any item reaches the terminal lane (`done`), a different agent than the one who built it must approve the work. This is enforced by the orchestrator, not by lane config.

**If no review lane exists in config.statuses:**
The orchestrator injects an inline review step automatically — picks the best-fit reviewer agent (different from assigned_to), invokes `backlog review`, waits for verdict. The item does not advance to done without a pass. No visible lane is created — this happens transparently in the orchestrator's execution.

**Config:**
```json
"orchestrator": {
  "require_review": true  // default
}
```
Setting `require_review: false` is allowed but Flow warns at startup:
> ⚠ require_review is disabled — items will reach done without peer review.

**Reviewer selection:**
Orchestrator picks the reviewer using the same agent_fit lens as the tribunal — highest skill match, lowest workload, has not worked on this item. If no suitable reviewer exists, item is flagged and human is notified.

**Acceptance criteria:**
- Item built by backend-dev cannot reach done without a different agent approving
- Board with no code-review lane still enforces inline review
- `require_review: false` disables the gate with a loud warning
- Reviewer is never the same agent who implemented the item
- No suitable reviewer available → human notified, item paused

links.reason: Orchestrator is responsible for enforcing this gate at every lane transition

items.title: Auto mode vs Supervised mode
items.description:
Two fundamentally different ways to run Flow — not a feature toggle but a product mode that changes who is in control.

**Supervised mode (default):**
Human controls when work starts. Items are refined and moved to `ready` by the human or lead agent with human approval. Once `ready`, the orchestrator takes over — drives execution, manages agents, processes results, handles review. Human re-engages only on genuine blockers or when an agent cannot proceed.

The human's role: decide what gets started. Not how it gets done.

**Auto mode:**
Lead agent owns the full cycle from backlog to done. It:
1. Runs tribunal to pick the highest-value item
2. Refines vague items — reads description, identifies gaps, asks the human targeted questions
3. Gets answers, completes refinement, moves item to ready
4. Assigns best-fit agent, initiates handoff
5. Drives through all lanes including review
6. Delivers completion summary to human

Human's role in auto mode: answer questions when the lead agent asks. That's it.

**Config:**
```json
"orchestrator": {
  "mode": "supervised"  // or "auto"
}
```

**Lead agent in auto mode:**
The lead agent is a special role — not a worker, not a reviewer. It reads the full backlog, understands the project context, makes prioritization decisions, and coordinates other agents. Configured in `config.orchestrator.lead_agent`.

**Question protocol in auto mode:**
When the lead agent hits ambiguity during refinement, it does not guess. It surfaces the minimum set of questions needed to proceed — max 2 at a time, most blocking first. Human answers, lead agent resumes. No back-and-forth beyond what's necessary.

**Acceptance criteria:**
- `config.orchestrator.mode: supervised` — human must move item to ready before orchestrator acts
- `config.orchestrator.mode: auto` — lead agent picks item, refines, asks human only when stuck, drives to done
- Mode is visible on the board (status indicator)
- Switching modes mid-flight does not disrupt in-progress items
- Auto mode never starts work on an item without completing refinement first

links.reason: Mode determines how the orchestrator decides when to initiate agents and when to pause for human input
Both modes must enforce the review gate — auto mode does it transparently, supervised mode does it explicitly

items.title: Graph view resets zoom/pan on background poll
items.description: Every background data refresh re-renders the graph diagram and resets the viewport zoom/pan position to fit-to-screen. This makes it impossible to explore a specific area of the graph after zooming in. Should preserve viewport transform state across polls and only reset on explicit user action (e.g. switching away from graph view and back).

items.title: Work brief → agent handoff
items.description:
Turn the scored work brief into an actual agent invocation via `backlog handoff <agent>` CLI command. The brief already exists via /api/pulse — this makes it executable.

**Mechanism**: Shell out to `claude` CLI subprocess with a structured prompt assembled from the pulse response: task title, description, linked items, scoring rationale, constraints, and agent persona from .claude/agents/<name>.md.

**Structured prompt must include**:
- Item details (title, description, tags, complexity, priority)
- Linked items with relationship type and reason
- Tribunal justification (why this item was picked, lens scores)
- Agent persona (skills, learnings from .claude/agents/<name>.md)
- Output contract: agent must produce a JSON completion report on finish

**JSON completion report schema** (stdout, parseable by #3 result ingestion):
```json
{
  "item_id": "a1b2c3d4",
  "status": "done" | "blocked" | "partial",
  "summary": "...",
  "bugs_found": [{"title": "...", "description": "..."}],
  "follow_ups": [{"title": "...", "description": "..."}],
  "blocker": "..." // if status=blocked
}
```

**Implementation plan**:
1. New CLI command: `backlog handoff <agent> [--item <pos>] [--dry-run]`
2. If --item not specified, use pulse to pick the top recommended item for that agent
3. Assemble structured prompt from item data + pulse response + agent persona
4. Shell out: `claude --print '<prompt>'` (non-interactive, capture output)
5. Parse JSON report from stdout, write to `handoff_results/<item_id>_<timestamp>.json`
6. On --dry-run, print the assembled prompt without invoking claude

**Acceptance criteria**:
- `backlog handoff backend-dev` picks top ready item, invokes claude CLI, saves result JSON
- `backlog handoff backend-dev --dry-run` prints assembled prompt to stdout
- `backlog handoff backend-dev --item 2` forces item #2 regardless of pulse recommendation
- Result JSON is valid and matches schema above
- Errors (claude not found, item not ready, no ready items) give clear messages

links.reason: Handoff must exist before result ingestion can receive anything back

items.title: Agent result ingestion
items.description:
When an agent finishes a task, Flow reads its JSON completion report from `handoff_results/` and automatically drives the backlog forward. This is the return path of the agentic loop — without it, handoff is a one-way street.

**Trigger:** Called by the orchestrator immediately after handoff completes. Also works standalone: `backlog ingest <result_file>` for manual or testing use.

**Result schema (defined by handoff):**
```json
{
  "item_id": "a1b2c3d4",
  "status": "done" | "blocked" | "partial",
  "summary": "...",
  "bugs_found": [{"title": "...", "description": "..."}],
  "follow_ups": [{"title": "...", "description": "..."}],
  "blocker": "..."  // if status=blocked
}
```

**Status handling:**

`done` → advance item to next lane (code-review if it exists, otherwise inline review gate, then done). Orchestrator initiates reviewer agent.

`blocked` → item stays in-progress. Dev agent opens a thread on the item tagging the lead agent (`waiting_on: "lead"`) with the blocker description. Lead agent reads it, either resolves the blocker directly or escalates to human with context. Human only sees it if lead cannot resolve it.

`partial` → item stays in-progress. Dev agent opens a thread tagging lead agent with what was completed and what remains. Lead agent clarifies the gap, updates the item description if needed, and re-initiates the dev agent with full context. Human escalation only if lead cannot determine how to proceed.

**Discovered items (bugs_found + follow_ups):**
- Each entry becomes a new backlog item appended to the bottom
- `bugs_found` → category: bug, priority_weight: 8 (default, adjustable)
- `follow_ups` → category: feature, priority_weight: 5 (default)
- Both linked back to the originating item with `discovered-during` type and a reason
- Tribunal re-runs after ingestion so new items are immediately scored and visible in pulse

**Score update:**
After ingestion, re-compute scores for all items affected by the state change — the completed item, any newly created items, and items it was blocking (unblock cascade).

**Acceptance criteria:**
- `backlog ingest handoff_results/a1b2c3d4_timestamp.json` processes the report correctly
- `done` → item advances to next lane, reviewer initiated
- `blocked` → thread opened on item, tagged lead, item stays in-progress
- `partial` → thread opened on item, tagged lead, item stays in-progress
- bugs_found and follow_ups become new backlog items with correct links and default scores
- Tribunal re-runs after ingestion
- Invalid or missing result file gives a clear error

links.reason: Handoff defines the result schema that ingestion parses
Orchestrator cannot close the loop without result ingestion processing agent outputs

items.title: Execution history per item
items.description: Store what actually happened on each item — not just status changes, but agent actions, findings, decisions made, and why. Answers 'why does #7 have a reopen penalty?' and makes the scoring model explainable. Foundation for feedback-aware re-scoring.

links.reason: Result ingestion produces the raw data that execution history organizes

items.title: Cold-start UX
items.description: A new user clones Flow and has no backlog.json, no context, no idea what to say. What happens? This item covers the first-time experience: guided setup, example prompts, empty-state messaging on the board, and making the first 'add item' feel natural. The product is only as good as its day-1 experience.

items.title: Feedback-aware re-scoring
items.description: After each agent execution, capture an outcome signal (success, partial, reopened, blocked) and feed it back into the scoring model. Simple at first — thumbs up/down or inferred from reopen/done status — but the foundation matters. Over time, Flow gets smarter about what it recommends.

links.reason: Execution history provides the signal that re-scoring learns from

items.title: Lane history: track actor on every transition
items.description:
Currently lane_history entries are bare strings for early moves (e.g. 'backlog', 'refined', 'ready') with no record of who made the transition or when. Only agent-driven moves include a structured object with `lane`, `at`, and `by` fields.

Every lane transition — whether done by a human, the CLI, or an agent — should record the same structured format:
```json
{"lane": "refined", "at": "<iso-timestamp>", "by": "<actor>"}
```

Actor should be: agent name if known, 'user' for CLI/board moves without an explicit actor, or the authenticated user if auth is added later.

Impact: fixes audit trail gap, enables 'who refined this?' queries, makes execution history (#4) accurate from day one.

links.reason: Execution history per item depends on accurate actor-stamped lane transitions

items.title: Code review assignment — who reviews agent work?
items.description:
When an agent moves an item to code-review, there is no mechanism to assign a reviewer or notify anyone. The item sits in code-review indefinitely with no owner.

Needs a solution for:
1. **Reviewer assignment**: `assigned_to` currently tracks the worker — we need a separate `reviewer` field (or a convention like `assigned_to` transfers on code-review entry)
2. **Notification / visibility**: reviewer should be surfaced in pulse, board, and CLI show output
3. **Policy hook**: a policy rule like `on: enter_lane(code-review) → assign_reviewer(lead)` would make this automatic

Decisions needed before refining: does reviewer replace or supplement assigned_to? Is 'lead' a special agent role or just a config value?

items.title: GitHub Issues import bridge
items.description: One-way import from GitHub Issues into the backlog (`backlog import --from github-issues`). Dramatically lowers adoption barrier — users don't need a clean JSON backlog to start. Maps issue labels/milestones to Flow tags/complexity where possible. Read-only sync to start.
